title: Rosie the Riveter Commemorative Coin Act
short_title: Rosie the Riveter Commemorative Coin Act
plain_summary: This bill tells the Treasury to make special coins in 2028 to honor the women who worked in factories and other jobs during World War Two. These women were called Rosie the Riveter and they helped America win the war by making weapons and supplies while men were fighting. The Treasury will make gold, silver, and half dollar coins with pictures celebrating these women. When people buy these coins, extra money from the sales will go to help take care of the Rosie the Riveter National Historical Park and teach people about what these brave women did.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Financial Services.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Rosie the Riveter Commemorative Coin Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Findings
sections.text: Congress has found these important facts about women during World War Two. More than six million American women started working during the war to fill jobs left empty when men joined the military. Millions more women who were already working switched to jobs that helped the war effort. These women worked full time or volunteered in factories, farms, shipyards, banks, and other places to support American soldiers overseas. They worked with groups that helped soldiers and drove streetcars, put airplane parts together, collected materials needed for the war, made bandages, and served on boards that decided how much food and supplies people could buy. These women workers met the growing need for war materials like equipment, weapons, ammunition, and food for soldiers. They also met the need for support services in schools, hospitals, and offices. All the American women who worked or volunteered during World War Two are represented by the famous image of Rosie the Riveter. As riveters, welders, and other workers, women from many different backgrounds played an important part in shipyards, airplane factories, and other places that made things for the military. Women workers faced a double burden of doing their jobs and taking care of their homes and families at the same time. These Rosies kept working even when they faced harassment, unfair treatment, and prejudice while supporting their country and families during a time of great need. Even though there were rules about equal pay, women were regularly paid ten to fifteen cents less per hour than men doing the same work. Black, Latina, Indigenous, Asian American, and Pacific Islander Rosies faced racist discrimination, violence, and harassment at work, at home, and in public. Women of color overcame long standing policies of discrimination and racial separation to make important contributions to the war effort and job market. Our Rosies show women's strength and the valuable contribution women made to the American war effort during World War Two, inspiring future generations of women and girls. Americans have chosen to honor the women who helped on the Home Front during World War Two by giving them a Congressional Gold Medal and celebrating National Rosie the Riveter Day on March twenty first each year. The work, sacrifice, and dedication of our Rosies deserve national reflection and remembrance. It is right and proper to recognize and preserve the history and legacy of American women who helped on the Home Front during World War Two for their patriotic commitment to their country and their perseverance when facing difficulties.

sections.title: Section 3 Coin Specifications
sections.text: To recognize and celebrate the American women who helped on the Home Front during World War Two, the Secretary of the Treasury shall make and issue these coins. The Secretary can make up to fifty thousand five dollar gold coins. Each coin will weigh eight point three five nine grams, have a width of zero point eight five zero inches, and contain at least ninety percent gold. The Secretary can make up to four hundred thousand one dollar silver coins. Each coin will weigh twenty six point seven three grams, have a width of one point five zero zero inches, and contain at least ninety percent silver. The Secretary can make up to seven hundred fifty thousand half dollar clad coins. Each coin will weigh eleven point three four grams, have a width of one point two zero five inches, and be made to the same specifications as other half dollar coins described in section five thousand one hundred twelve of title thirty one of the United States Code. The coins made under this law shall be legal tender, as provided in section five thousand one hundred three of title thirty one of the United States Code. For the purposes of sections five thousand one hundred thirty four and five thousand one hundred thirty six of title thirty one of the United States Code, all coins made under this law shall be considered to be collectible items.

sections.title: Section 4 Designs of Coins
sections.text: The designs of the coins made under this law shall represent the legacy of the diverse women workforce who helped on the Home Front during World War Two. On each coin made under this law there shall be a marking showing the value of the coin, the year two thousand twenty eight written on it, and the words Liberty, In God We Trust, United States of America, and E Pluribus Unum written on it. The designs of the coins made under this law shall be chosen by the Secretary after talking with the Rosie the Riveter Trust and the Commission of Fine Arts. The designs will also be reviewed by the Citizens Coinage Advisory Committee.

sections.title: Section 5 Issuance of Coins
sections.text: Coins made under this law shall be issued in uncirculated and proof qualities. The Secretary may issue coins made under this law only during the one year period beginning on January first, two thousand twenty eight.

sections.title: Section 6 Sale of Coins
sections.text: The coins issued under this law shall be sold by the Secretary at a price equal to the face value of the coins plus the surcharge provided in section seven with respect to such coins plus the cost of designing and issuing the coins including labor, materials, dies, use of machinery, overhead expenses, marketing, and shipping. The Secretary shall make bulk sales of the coins issued under this law at a reasonable discount. The Secretary shall accept prepaid orders for the coins made under this law before the coins are issued. Sale prices for prepaid orders shall be at a reasonable discount.

sections.title: Section 7 Surcharges
sections.text: All sales of coins made under this law shall include a surcharge of thirty five dollars per coin for the five dollar coins, a surcharge of ten dollars per coin for the one dollar coins, and a surcharge of five dollars per coin for the half dollar coins. Subject to section five thousand one hundred thirty four of title thirty one of the United States Code, all surcharges received by the Secretary from the sale of coins issued under this law shall be promptly paid by the Secretary to the Rosie the Riveter Trust to support the National Park Service in maintaining and repairing the Rosie the Riveter World War Two Home Front National Historical Park and for educational and commemorative programs. The Rosie the Riveter Trust shall be subject to audit requirements of section five thousand one hundred thirty four of title thirty one of the United States Code regarding the amounts received. Despite the surcharge requirements, no surcharge may be included for any coin issued under this law during a calendar year if issuing such coin would result in more than two commemorative coin programs being issued during that year, which is the annual limit under section five thousand one hundred twelve of title thirty one of the United States Code as it exists when this law is enacted. The Secretary of the Treasury may issue guidance to carry out this rule.

sections.title: Section 8 Financial Assurances
sections.text: The Secretary shall take actions necessary to ensure that making and issuing coins under this law will not result in any net cost to the United States Government. No funds, including applicable surcharges, shall be given to any recipient designated in section seven until the total cost of designing and issuing all of the coins authorized by this law is recovered by the United States Treasury. This total cost includes labor, materials, dies, use of machinery, winning design compensation, overhead expenses, marketing, and shipping, consistent with sections five thousand one hundred twelve and five thousand one hundred thirty four of title thirty one of the United States Code.
